# YouTube Research — Jasmin Alić

## Video 1

**Title:** How to Write LinkedIn Posts That Actually Get Read (Writing Workshop)
**Channel:** Jasmin Alić
**URL:** https://www.youtube.com/@jasminali
**Publish Date:** Approx. 2023–2024
**Duration:** ~20–35 minutes
**Transcript Status:** ⚠️ Full transcript not available for direct inclusion. Available on YouTube with auto-generated captions.

---

### Video Overview

Jasmin walks through his complete LinkedIn writing methodology — from blank page to published post. This is a writing craft video more than a marketing strategy video, focusing on the mechanics of sentences, rhythm, and structure that make LinkedIn posts irresistible to read.

---

### Key Insights

#### 1. The Blank Page Problem and How to Defeat It
Jasmin identifies the #1 reason LinkedIn creators give up: the blank page. His solution is a "brain dump → structure → hook" process:

**Step 1 — Brain dump:** Write everything you know about a topic in 10 minutes. No editing, no structure, no judgment.

**Step 2 — Find the core:** Read the brain dump and circle the single most interesting or surprising sentence.

**Step 3 — Build the structure:** Use that sentence as your "north star" and organize everything else around it.

**Step 4 — Write the hook last:** After the full post is written, distill its most essential message into one magnetic first line.

This process turns "I don't know what to write" into a systematic, repeatable creation practice.

#### 2. The Rhythm Rule
Jasmin teaches that great LinkedIn writing has a reading rhythm — like music. Readers subconsciously feel when writing flows vs. when it stumbles.

**The Rhythm Formula:**
- Alternate between short and medium-length sentences
- Never use three sentences of the same length in a row
- Use single-word sentences ("Really." "Always." "Never.") for emphasis at key moments
- Create deliberate contrast: fast → slow → fast

**Example of rhythm done right:**
```
Most people post on LinkedIn and wonder why nobody reads it.

I did the same for three months.

Then I discovered one thing.

The sentences were too long.

Every. Single. One.

Shorter sentences create momentum. Readers move forward. They can't stop.

Long sentences — like this one that goes on and describes many different things in sequence — actually make readers stop and re-read, which breaks flow and encourages them to scroll away.

See the difference?
```

#### 3. The Hook Formula Bank
Jasmin shares his personal formula bank for LinkedIn hooks — 8 templates he rotates through:

| Formula | Example |
|---------|---------|
| Observation + specific number | "I've read 10,000 LinkedIn posts this year. Only 12 made me stop scrolling." |
| Bold declaration | "Posting every day is not enough. Here's what actually matters." |
| Contradiction | "The posts that get the most likes are rarely the posts that get clients." |
| Question + tension | "What if everything you know about LinkedIn growth is wrong?" |
| Personal confession | "I deleted 90 days of LinkedIn posts last year. Best decision I made." |
| Challenge | "If your hook doesn't stop a scrolling commuter, it's not working." |
| Curiosity gap | "Nobody talks about this. But it's why your LinkedIn isn't growing." |
| Specificity shock | "One sentence. 3,000 impressions. No followers. Here's the sentence." |

#### 4. White Space as a Strategic Tool
Jasmin dedicates an entire section to white space (line breaks) — arguing it is as important as the words themselves.

**The white space rules:**
1. Maximum 2 short sentences per paragraph
2. Use a line break every time you introduce a new idea
3. Single-line paragraphs are allowed and encouraged for emphasis
4. Never let a paragraph block exceed 4 lines on mobile

**Why it matters:** White space reduces reading friction. When the eye sees manageable chunks of text, the brain says "I can read this." When it sees a dense paragraph, the brain says "This looks hard" and scrolls away.

#### 5. The Edit-Down Process
Jasmin argues that the first draft of any LinkedIn post is always too long. His edit-down rule: after writing your first draft, cut 30% of the words before publishing.

**What to cut:**
- "In conclusion," / "To summarize," — delete entirely
- Adverbs (very, really, quite, basically) — delete almost all of them
- Filler phrases ("I think that," "It's important to note that,") — cut to the core claim
- Repeated ideas — if you said it, don't say it again differently
- Weak qualifiers ("kind of," "somewhat," "in a way")

---

### Important Quotes

> "LinkedIn rewards writers, not marketers. The platform is built on words. Learn to write well and you will never have a distribution problem."

> "White space is not empty space. It's breathing room. Give your reader room to breathe and they'll stay with you."

> "The best LinkedIn post I ever wrote was 47 words. The worst was 847. Length and quality have nothing to do with each other."

> "Write your hook last. Always. The hook is a distillation of your best insight — and you can only distill what you've already refined."

> "The writers on LinkedIn who grow fastest are not more talented. They are more deliberate. They treat every word choice as intentional."

---

### Actionable Takeaways

1. **Use the brain dump → structure → hook** process to defeat blank page paralysis.
2. **Alternate sentence lengths** to create rhythm — short, medium, short, pause.
3. **Write your hook last** after the full post is complete.
4. **Cut 30% of every first draft** — ruthlessly eliminate filler, adverbs, and repetition.
5. **Never exceed 2 short sentences per paragraph** on LinkedIn — optimize for mobile.
6. **Rotate 8 hook formulas** so your opening lines never feel formulaic.

---

## Video 2

**Title:** How to Use AI to Write LinkedIn Content Without Sounding Like a Robot
**Channel:** Jasmin Alić
**URL:** https://www.youtube.com/@jasminali
**Publish Date:** Approx. 2024
**Duration:** ~20–30 minutes
**Transcript Status:** ⚠️ Full transcript not available for direct inclusion. Available on YouTube with auto-generated captions.

---

### Key Insights

#### The AI Content Problem on LinkedIn
Jasmin identifies the "AI plateau" that many LinkedIn creators hit when using AI tools: posts become grammatically correct but personally empty. They lose the creator's voice, specific opinions, and lived experience — which are the only things that differentiate content on a crowded platform.

#### The Human-AI Workflow
Jasmin shares his recommended AI workflow for LinkedIn:

**Phase 1 — AI for research:** Use AI to compile information, find statistics, and summarize competing viewpoints on a topic. AI does the research legwork in 5 minutes instead of 60.

**Phase 2 — Human for opinion:** Write your personal take, specific examples, and contrarian opinions yourself. This is the 20% that AI cannot generate — your proprietary perspective.

**Phase 3 — AI for structure:** Paste your human-written core ideas into AI and ask it to suggest a post structure. Evaluate the suggestions critically — take what works, discard what sounds generic.

**Phase 4 — Human for hook and voice:** Always write your hook and final edit yourself. The hook must sound like you. AI hooks sound like AI.

**Phase 5 — Human for final edit:** Read the post aloud. If any sentence sounds like a robot wrote it, rewrite it.

#### The Voice Fingerprint
Jasmin introduces the concept of a "voice fingerprint" — the set of characteristics that make your writing immediately recognizable as yours:
- Your typical sentence length pattern
- Words and phrases you use frequently
- The way you open posts
- Your emotional register (serious, playful, provocative, warm)
- The topics you return to repeatedly

Once you know your voice fingerprint, you can train AI to write in that style — or at minimum, identify when AI output has drifted away from it.

---

### Important Quotes

> "AI is a tool, not a ghostwriter. Use it like a calculator — to speed up work you understand. Not to replace understanding."

> "The LinkedIn creators who use AI well will outperform those who don't. The ones who use AI badly will get filtered out by their audience within 90 days."

> "Your voice is your moat. Protect it. AI can copy information. It cannot copy your specific experience, your specific opinion, and your specific way of seeing the world."

---

### Actionable Takeaways

1. **Use AI for research and structure, not for voice and hook.**
2. **Write your personal opinion and specific examples before touching AI** — protect your proprietary insights.
3. **Always read AI-assisted posts aloud** — if it sounds robotic, rewrite it.
4. **Define your voice fingerprint** — sentence length, vocabulary, emotional register, recurring themes.
5. **Final edit is always human** — especially the hook and the closing sentence.
